text: Hmm, yeah sorry for creating the confusion here. I'm going to try and summarize my thoughts and what I hope to gain as follows: if we replace the mutable state in OOP/actors with versioned state, could our reasoning, live programming, time travel etc. be as good as we get with FP? Could we get the same benefits for large, distributed systems as well? Anyway, this is probably not a great medium for this discussion further. I hope to write up some more detail at some point.

Interesting work from Cloudflare in running customer computations using V8: <https://blog.cloudflare.com/cloud-computing-without-containers/> I tend to believe that the sheer amount of investment in browser execution contexts will make JS/WASM the most interesting platform on which to build future computing models

attachments.title: Cloud Computing without Containers
attachments.text: We have a cloud computing platform called Workers. Unlike essentially every other cloud computing platform I know of, it doesn’t use containers or virtual machines. We believe that is the future of Serverless and cloud computing in general, and I’ll try to convince you why.
attachments.fallback: The Cloudflare Blog: Cloud Computing without Containers

Documentation is a lousy way to foster collaboration.

And all the up-front design really discourages collaboration. If the next person can't make any improvements, they have no opportunity to make it their own. This is where compatibility really kills you. Either you have to get it right up front, or you get stuck supporting crap. Either way: no collaboration.

One thing that's happening is the shader pipeline is gaining a lot more complicity, ray tracing along adds 6 new shaders (callable, closest hit, any hit, intersection, miss, ray generation) <https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/desktop/direct3d12/direct3d-12-raytracing-hlsl-shaders>

attachments.title: Direct3D 12 Raytracing HLSL Shaders
attachments.text: The following HLSL shaders support the Direct3D 12 raytracing pipeline.

text: that doesn't include non-raytracing features like mesh shaders and texture space shading
So a trend in graphics programming has been having the GPU generate work\spawn tasks for itself

meanwhile there is more fixed function or special purpose hardware available (RT cores, Tensorcores) on RTX, I kind of want a system where there are no explicit shader stages, you just have "shaders" that can access fixed function and special purpose hardware (texture units, rasterizer, ROPs, RT Core, Tensor Core, etc.) and dispatch new shaders

attachments.title: Vulkan Device-Generated Commands
attachments.text: We are introducing a device generated command (DGC) Vulkan extension, which allows the GPU to generate the most frequent rendering commands on its own. This means for the first time an open graphics API provides functionality to create compact command buffer streams on the device avoiding the worst-case state setup of previous indirect drawing methods.

Yeah, I should elaborate. Well-written documentation can be _great_ for collaboration. But well-written documentation can be _really_ hard to do, _even_ when the incentives are perfectly aligned (very rare even in open source; the world is complex). So lately I try to at least talk about the thing right in front of the thing rather than on some other page all by itself. That way at least if I screw up how I describe the thing, at least the reader has a chance at trying to cross-triangulate what I mean by looking at and running the thing itself.

But you're a Smalltalk guy. I suspect I'm preaching to the choir :slightly_smiling_face:

I'm really excited about the WHLSL news out of WebKit (<https://webkit.org/blog/8482/web-high-level-shading-language/>). Looks like the WebGPU project is maturing. Not quite as close to the metal as the RTX stuff mentioned above, but exciting for the sake of bringing a low-overhead graphics API to the masses, and putting it within reach of web developers (as opposed to game/app developers).

For the _future of coding_ angle on this <#CE1R695T7|graphics> stuff, my company does a lot of interactive graphics, and I'm working on a visual programming language to support some of that. It's a nice domain to be in, since there's a tighter connection between the language and the result than there would be if (say) I were making a language for correctness validation or serving web apps. I'd be excited to hear if anyone else here is working on/with tools/languages that have a strong component of graphics to them.
